narrative:
Robert Brown Aderholt has represented Alabama's 4th Congressional District since January 1997, making him one of the longest-serving members of the House and, by nearly any measure, one of the most institutionally embedded[1]. A former municipal judge and aide to Governor Fob James before flipping a longtime Democratic seat in 1996 by a razor-thin margin[2], Aderholt has since made himself almost entirely irrelevant to the electoral calendar — running unopposed in multiple cycles and winning his 2024 race with 98.8% of the vote[3]. He now chairs the House Appropriations Subcommittee on Labor, Health and Human Services, and Education — the largest non-defense slice of the federal budget — while also sitting on the Defense and Agriculture Subcommittees[4]. In Washington's power architecture, that is a formidable perch. What happens from that perch, however, is where the 80% Bill becomes instructive.

On nearly every plank of the 80% Bill, Aderholt's record ranges from quiet opposition to active obstruction. He voted against certifying the Electoral College results from Arizona and Pennsylvania on January 6, 2021[3], putting him squarely against the fair-elections consensus. The For the People Act of 2021, which would have addressed gerrymandering and expanded voting access, passed the House with all present Republicans voting no[5] — Aderholt among them. On taxes, he signed the Taxpayer Protection Pledge, voted to repeal the estate tax[7], and voted for the "One Big Beautiful Bill" in 2025, which delivered the largest tax cuts in a generation while cutting Medicaid — a piece of legislation Aderholt praised as delivering "tax relief, a secure border, and a government that lives within its means"[8]. The bill's Medicaid cuts affect the very constituents populating the rural Alabama counties he has represented for three decades.

His record on healthcare is similarly clarifying. As chairman of the Labor-HHS subcommittee, Aderholt has direct jurisdiction over federal health spending, yet he has consistently opposed Medicare's ability to negotiate drug prices[9]. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, which passed along party lines and finally gave Medicare that negotiating authority[10]. On Social Security, Aderholt led a subcommittee markup in 2024 that proposed cutting the Social Security Administration's administrative budget by $450 million — roughly a 4% reduction — which Democrats and union officials warned would accelerate field office closures and extend already brutal wait times for beneficiaries[11].

Follow the money and the geometry sharpens. Aderholt raised $1.5 million for his 2024 campaign despite running essentially uncontested[12]. His committee assignments — overseeing the budgets for defense, agriculture, and all of HHS — make him a prime target for PAC contributions from the very industries he regulates. Northrop Grumman personally thanked Aderholt for his "advocacy" in opening a $20 million battle command facility in North Alabama[13], and Aderholt has secured millions in federal appropriations for Redstone Arsenal and other military installations in his district[14]. He chairs the Values Action Team, a congressional group focused on "families, communities, and religious freedom" — a framing that notably excludes paid family leave, a policy backed by supermajorities of Americans, and one Aderholt has never championed in nearly three decades in office.

Jake Auchincloss is a Harvard-educated, MIT-MBA-holding Marine veteran who represents Massachusetts's 4th District — a resume so aggressively optimized it reads like a LinkedIn profile designed by McKinsey. Elected in 2020 at the age of 32 to succeed Joe Kennedy III, Auchincloss arrived in Congress with a background spanning infantry command in Afghanistan, cybersecurity startups, and a stint at Liberty Mutual's innovation lab [1]. He has since positioned himself as the Democratic Party's foremost techno-optimist, a congressman who believes artificial intelligence will save civilization and who proved it by becoming the first member of Congress to deliver a floor speech written entirely by ChatGPT [2]. The speech advocated for a U.S.-Israel AI research center, which is to say he outsourced his job to a machine and used the savings to lobby for more machines.

On the 80% Bill's twenty priorities, Auchincloss presents a genuinely interesting case study in selective populism. He co-sponsored the Bipartisan Ban on Congressional Stock Ownership Act, publicly declaring that members of Congress should sacrifice the ability to own individual equities — a position that would be more striking if his own financial disclosures did not reveal a conveniently timed sale of State Street Corporation stock shortly before the firm announced a billion-dollar debt issuance [3]. His net worth, estimated between $1.5 million and $9 million according to his 2023 disclosure, suggests he would survive the restriction [4]. He supports Medicare drug negotiation and paid family leave, but his enthusiasm for taxing the ultra-wealthy and banning corporate PACs has been notably more muted, placing him in that comfortable centrist lane where you endorse popular ideas loudly enough to get credit but quietly enough to keep the fundraising emails flowing.

Where Auchincloss gets genuinely interesting — or genuinely frustrating, depending on your view — is on technology and defense. He has argued passionately that Congress must regulate AI before it becomes another social media debacle, insisting that large language models are "prediction engines, not intelligence" and that the country needs a "public counterweight" to Big Tech [5]. This is commendable, except that his primary legislative vehicle for AI governance is more research funding rather than the kind of structural reform that might actually constrain the companies whose PACs populate his donor rolls. On defense, the former Marine supports ending forever wars in principle but has not been a leading voice on Pentagon audits — perhaps understandable for someone whose military service gives him genuine credibility but also genuine institutional loyalty [6].

Auchincloss's record on the remaining planks is a mixed bag that tracks with his New Democrat Coalition positioning. He backs fair elections reform and the DISCLOSE Act, supports police body cameras, and has voiced support for right-to-repair legislation — all sensible, broadly popular, low-cost positions [7]. On the more structurally challenging items like term limits, banning corporate home buying, and funding Social Security through higher contributions from top earners, he has been quieter. His 2026 campaign literature emphasizes "affordability and innovation," a framing that conveniently avoids committing to the redistributive measures that would make affordability more than a slogan. In a district that spans wealthy Newton suburbs and working-class Taunton, Auchincloss has mastered the art of sounding progressive on technology while remaining fundamentally moderate on money [8].

Mark Amodei has represented Nevada's 2nd District since winning a 2011 special election, and in February 2026 he announced he would not seek re-election [1]. Before arriving in Congress, Amodei spent a career steeped in the industries that would later fund his campaigns: he served as President of the Nevada Mining Association from 2007 to 2008 [2], a credential that pairs nicely with a seat on the House Natural Resources Committee, where he oversees the very federal lands his former employers want to dig up.

Amodei sits on the powerful House Appropriations Committee, including the Homeland Security Subcommittee he chaired beginning in 2024 [3]. From that perch he helps decide how hundreds of billions of taxpayer dollars are spent -- yet he has never co-sponsored legislation to audit the Pentagon, an agency that has failed seven consecutive audits and cannot account for 63 percent of its nearly $4 trillion in assets [4]. Defense contractors based in his district, like AMAD Inc., have won sole-source contracts worth over $1 million while their lobbyists donate to his campaigns [5].

In 2024, his top contributing industry was Casinos and Gambling at $101,942, with large individual contributions making up 52.88% of his war chest and PAC money accounting for another 43.19% [6]. Early donors over his career include AT&T, Exxon Mobil, General Dynamics, General Electric, and UPS [7]. Small-dollar donors -- the kind who actually live paycheck to paycheck -- barely register.

In May 2025, Amodei co-sponsored an amendment authorizing the sale or exchange of up to 450,000 acres of Nevada public lands across Clark, Washoe, Lyon, and Pershing counties, with proceeds funneled to the U.S. Treasury to offset federal tax cuts [8]. To his credit, he was one of only four House Republicans to vote against a $9.4 billion rescissions package backed by DOGE in June 2025, calling it fiscally irresponsible [9]. That lone act of defiance, however, does not erase a career spent shepherding public resources toward private interests.

Amodei has not co-sponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, impose term limits with teeth, ban corporate PACs, or close the carried-interest loophole. He voted against the For the People Act (H.R. 1) and has shown no interest in the DISCLOSE Act [10]. He is retiring with his mining-industry Rolodex intact and no lobbying ban in sight -- which may be the whole point.

Alma S. Adams has represented North Carolina's 12th Congressional District since 2014, when she won a special election and was sworn in as the 100th woman in the 113th Congress [1]. A former professor at Bennett College in Greensboro, Adams earned a Ph.D. in art education and brought an academic's rigor to the House, where she sits on the Education & the Workforce and Agriculture committees [2]. She is fondly known as the "Godmother of HBCUs" for co-founding and chairing the Congressional Bipartisan HBCU Caucus, and she shepherded the FUTURE Act into law, permanently directing $255 million a year to minority-serving institutions [3]. In Charlotte's NC-12, she has faced little electoral trouble, which gives her the luxury of picking her battles rather than running from them.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Adams checks a remarkable number of boxes. She voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included the first-ever Medicare drug price negotiation program [4]. She championed paid family leave as part of the Build Back Better Act, publicly commending House leadership for its inclusion [5]. She co-sponsored the For the People Act (H.R. 1), a sweeping package covering fair elections, dark money disclosure, and anti-corruption measures -- and authored amendments requiring the Postal Service to conduct daily ballot sweeps during elections and rewarding colleges that excel in voter registration [6]. She co-sponsored the bipartisan TRUST in Congress Act to ban members and their families from trading individual stocks [7].

Where Adams falls quieter is on the issues that lack a ready Democratic caucus vehicle. There is no public record of her leading the charge on a Pentagon audit, a lobbying ban for former members, or legislation to stop corporate purchases of single-family homes. She has not introduced or co-sponsored a term limits amendment. On these items, silence is the position -- and silence, in a body that runs on cosponsorship, is a choice.

Campaign finance tells a familiar Democratic story. Adams raised roughly $1.1 million for 2024, with a mix of individual donors and PAC contributions [8]. Her top industry donors include leadership PACs and labor unions, plus contributions from the real estate and health sectors [9]. None of it is scandalous by Washington standards, but none of it is small-dollar populist fuel either. She has not sworn off corporate PAC money, even as she supports disclosure reforms that would make such money more transparent.

The bottom line: Adams is a reliable progressive vote on health care, education, and election reform, with genuine legislative wins to show for it. But reliability and ambition are different animals. On the handful of 80% Bill issues that would ruffle institutional feathers -- auditing the Pentagon, banning lobbying revolving doors, ending corporate home purchases -- she has kept her powder conspicuously dry. The Godmother of HBCUs has plenty of political capital; the question is whether she will spend it beyond her comfort zone.

Pete Aguilar, Democrat of California's 33rd district and Chair of the House Democratic Caucus since 2023, is one of the most powerful Democrats in the House. A former mayor of Redlands first elected to Congress in 2014, Aguilar has spent a decade climbing the leadership ladder, which makes his relationship with the 80% Bill a study in the difference between what party leaders say at press conferences and what they actually push when the cameras turn off [1][2].

On paper, Aguilar checks many of the right boxes. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation, a provision supported by roughly 90% of Americans [3]. He co-sponsored the For the People Act (HR 1), which would have addressed gerrymandering, dark money, and campaign finance reform in one sweeping package, and he voted for its passage in both the 116th and 117th Congresses [4][5]. He supported the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act, which included body camera requirements for federal officers [6]. And he voted for the Epstein Files Transparency Act in November 2025, even publicly needling Trump for trying to slow-walk the release [7]. On Social Security, he launched a Task Force on Aging and Families to defend benefits against Republican cuts [8].

But here is where it gets interesting. On congressional stock trading, the issue that unites roughly 86% of voters across party lines, Aguilar has played the cautious leader. When a bipartisan bill emerged that would require lawmakers to fully divest individual stocks, Aguilar offered the tepid endorsement that he "wishes it would have been a more robust package" while stopping short of championing the cause himself [9]. According to Quiver Quantitative, Aguilar holds approximately $0 in publicly traded assets, which means he has less personal skin in the game than many colleagues yet still will not lead on the issue [10]. On term limits, the lifetime lobbying ban, and auditing the Pentagon, his record is largely one of silence, which is itself a position when you chair the caucus that could bring these issues to the floor.

Follow the money and the silence starts to make more sense. In the 2023-2024 cycle, 43% of Aguilar's campaign funds came from PACs [11]. His career top contributors include the Credit Union National Association, J Street PAC, and the League of Conservation Voters [12]. He sits on the Appropriations Committee, including the Defense subcommittee, a perch that makes him extraordinarily attractive to lobbyists and defense contractors alike [13]. He also runs the Aguilar Leadership Fund, a leadership PAC that lets him distribute money to fellow Democrats and consolidate influence, the kind of institutional machinery that does not exactly incentivize rocking the boat on corporate PAC bans or the DISCLOSE Act [14].

The bottom line on Pete Aguilar is that he is a reliable Democratic vote on marquee legislation when it reaches the floor, but a conspicuously quiet leader on the structural reforms that would change how Congress itself operates. He will vote for drug pricing reform after it is already a done deal, but he will not stick his neck out on banning stock trades or corporate PACs, the very reforms that would diminish the leadership leverage he has spent a career building. For a man whose official title is "Chair of the Democratic Caucus," the 80% Bill reveals a familiar Washington paradox: the people with the most power to change the system are the ones with the least incentive to do so [15].

Rick W. Allen has represented Georgia's 12th Congressional District since 2015, bringing to Washington the sensibilities of a construction company owner from Augusta who spent three decades building things before deciding to legislate about them. A reliable Republican vote on the House Education and Labor Committee and the House Agriculture Committee, Allen ran on the familiar small-government playbook: cut regulations, lower taxes, and trust the free market to sort out the rest [1]. He has been reelected comfortably in his deep-red district ever since, rarely facing the kind of competitive race that might force him to reckon with what his constituents actually want on a policy-by-policy basis.

On the twenty issues that compose the 80% Bill, Allen's record is a study in selective populism. He has voiced support for Congressional term limits, a position that costs him nothing since the Republican conference has never seriously pushed it to a vote [2]. He backed the FARM Act provisions to scrutinize foreign purchases of U.S. farmland, aligning with the broad bipartisan consensus on protecting American agricultural land [3]. But on the items that would actually disturb the comfortable arrangements of Washington -- banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, requiring the DISCLOSE Act -- Allen has been conspicuously absent. He voted against the For the People Act, which contained anti-gerrymandering and campaign finance transparency provisions [4]. He has not cosponsored any legislation to ban members of Congress from trading individual stocks, nor has he shown interest in a lifetime lobbying ban for former members.

Where Allen truly parts company with his own voters is on kitchen-table economics. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation -- a provision supported by roughly 80% of Americans, including Republican voters [5]. He has opposed paid family leave mandates and has not championed right-to-repair legislation, despite representing a district full of farmers who would benefit from the right to fix their own John Deere tractors. On the Pentagon, Allen has consistently voted for defense spending increases without supporting serious audit requirements, even as the Department of Defense has failed its annual audit every single year since Congress first required one in 2018 [6].

Follow the money, and Allen's positions come into sharper focus. His top industry donors include the construction industry, real estate, and agribusiness, with significant contributions from corporate PACs [7]. The National Association of Home Builders, Associated Builders and Contractors, and various real estate interests have been among his most reliable financial backers. He has received substantial support from health sector PACs, which may help explain his reluctance to embrace Medicare drug negotiation. His career fundraising has leaned heavily on PAC money rather than small-dollar donations -- the very funding model that the 80% Bill's provisions on corporate PACs and dark money are designed to disrupt [8].

The bottom line on Rick Allen is familiar enough: a congressman whose populist instincts extend exactly as far as his donor base will allow. He will happily talk about term limits at a town hall and then return to Washington to vote in lockstep with the interests that fund his campaigns. His district may be safely red, but the 80% Bill's proposals are not partisan -- they are popular. Allen's challenge is not his voters; it is whether he can afford to listen to them.

Jodey Arrington chairs the House Budget Committee, which means he is technically the man in charge of making sure America does not spend more money than it has. He has held this position since 2023, during which time the national debt has continued its cheerful sprint toward oblivion. But Arrington is a "fiscal hawk," you see, which in Washington means you give impassioned speeches about the deficit while voting for every tax cut that inflates it. He was the principal author of the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act," the 2025 reconciliation package that made permanent the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act — a law that handed massive breaks to corporations and the ultra-wealthy while adding roughly $1.9 trillion to the deficit over a decade. The man tasked with balancing the books helped write the bill that unbalances them. You truly cannot make this stuff up.

Arrington represents Texas's 19th District, which he proudly calls the "Food, Fuel, and Fiber Capital of the World" — home to massive oil and gas production, cattle ranches, and the largest cotton patch on Earth. His top industry donors over his career include oil and gas, crop production, and agriculture services. He sits on the Ways and Means Committee, the most powerful tax-writing body in Congress, where he can conveniently shape the tax code for the industries that fund his campaigns. When it comes to energy policy, Arrington is a full-throated champion of fossil fuels who has opposed climate and clean energy mandates, even praising the House for restricting "Biden-era climate and social justice mandates in retirement investment."

On Social Security, Arrington has taken the approach of a man holding a fire extinguisher while also pouring gasoline. He warns constantly that Social Security faces "insolvency" and pushed for a bipartisan fiscal commission — which critics have called a vehicle to cut benefits behind closed doors while avoiding voter accountability. He has not, notably, proposed lifting the payroll tax cap to make the wealthy pay their fair share into the system, which is the actual popular solution supported by roughly 80 percent of Americans.

To his credit, Arrington co-chairs the Congressional Term Limits Caucus and authored bipartisan term-limit legislation endorsed by President Trump. That is a genuine alignment with the 80% Bill. But he is now retiring after just five terms, which either proves term limits work or suggests he got what he came for. He missed 4.4 percent of roll call votes during his tenure — more than double the median for sitting representatives. He voted to overturn the 2020 election results in Arizona and Pennsylvania. He has no notable record on banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, banning corporate PACs, or auditing the Pentagon. On the 80% Bill scorecard, Arrington talks about fiscal responsibility the way a pyromaniac talks about fire safety — with great passion and absolutely no credibility.

Mark Alford spent 23 years as a morning news anchor at Fox 4 in Kansas City before deciding the real performance art was in Congress. Elected to represent Missouri's 4th District in 2022, the former TV personality traded his teleprompter for a committee gavel, landing seats on Armed Services, Agriculture, and later Appropriations [1][2]. He ran as a Christian conservative who felt stifled by the media -- a curious complaint from a man who spent two decades on television -- and now represents a sprawling, agriculture-heavy district stretching from the Kansas City suburbs to the Ozarks [3].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Alford delivers a genuinely mixed bag. He deserves credit where it is due: he introduced the PELOSI Act, a bill to ban members of Congress and their spouses from trading individual stocks, calling out colleagues who engage in "suspicious stock trades based on non-public information" [4][5]. He signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge, supporting congressional term limits [6]. He voted to release the Epstein files, calling transparency "a moral issue" when the House passed the Epstein Files Transparency Act 427-1 in November 2025 [7][8]. And he introduced the AFIDA Improvements Act to strengthen oversight of foreign adversaries buying American farmland, a legitimate concern given his district's agricultural backbone [9].

But the populist streak runs dry when the bills threaten Republican orthodoxy. Alford floated cutting Medicare and Social Security as targets for DOGE-style government efficiency, suggesting the retirement age could be "moved back a little bit" -- a position that drew boos at his own town hall [10][11][12]. He voted for the Fiscal Year 2025 NDAA, hiking defense spending by $9 billion despite the Pentagon failing its seventh consecutive audit [13]. On Medicare drug negotiation, paid family leave, taxing the ultra-wealthy, banning corporate PACs, ending gerrymandering, and the DISCLOSE Act, Alford has been either silent or opposed -- the predictable posture of a Republican who will champion stock-trading bans but won't touch the donor class's real priorities.

Follow the money and the silences make sense. In the 2024 cycle, 46% of Alford's campaign funds came from PACs, with crop production and basic processing leading the industry pack at $84,625 [14][15]. The Missouri Farm Bureau PAC endorsed him, and defense contractors keep the contributions flowing to their man on Armed Services and Appropriations [16]. He co-founded the Bipartisan Congressional Real Estate Caucus, a nice perk for a former ReeceNichols realtor whose industry would rather not see a ban on corporate purchases of single-family homes [17]. AIPAC bundled nearly $27,000 for his campaign on top of a $5,000 direct contribution [15].

The bottom line on Mark Alford is that he is a congressman who has found a few populist positions that play well on camera -- stock trading bans, Epstein transparency, farmland protection -- while reliably voting the party line on everything that would actually redistribute power or money. He'll champion term limits while cashing PAC checks from the industries he oversees, and he'll talk about government waste while voting to increase the Pentagon's budget. The anchor desk taught him how to deliver the news people want to hear; Congress taught him how to vote the way donors want him to.

Gabe Amo arrived in Congress the way all great populists do: through the revolving door. Before representing Rhode Island's 1st District, Amo spent years as a registered lobbyist for Home Depot -- yes, the same Home Depot that was one of the most significant corporate funders of Trump Republicans. He then bounced between the Obama and Biden White Houses, mastering the art of sounding progressive while keeping powerful friends comfortable.

During his 2023 special election, the Working Families Party pulled back the curtain on Amo's donor rolodex: $21,127 from federal lobbyists representing Fox Corporation, Eli Lilly, Philip Morris, Marathon Petroleum, Blackstone, Bank of America, JPMorgan Chase, and Mastercard. When your campaign piggy bank reads like a Fortune 500 index fund, it gets harder to pretend you're fighting for the little guy.

To his credit, Amo signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge, supporting a constitutional amendment to cap House service at six years. It's a nice gesture from a man who spent his entire pre-congressional career learning how to be useful to the permanent government. He sits on the Foreign Affairs, Science, and Budget committees -- assignments that let him talk about accountability without having to vote on the domestic policies that might upset his donor base.

On the 80% Bill's twenty policies, Amo's record is a masterclass in strategic ambiguity. He hasn't cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act or legislation to ban corporate PACs. There's no record of him pushing to audit the Pentagon, despite sitting on the Budget Committee where that conversation would actually matter. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act's Medicare drug negotiation provisions, but that was a party-line layup that required zero courage.

His opponent in the 2023 primary, Aaron Regunberg, pledged to reject all corporate PAC and lobbyist money. Amo's campaign responded by pointing out that his donors also gave to Sheldon Whitehouse and Joe Biden -- as if that somehow made lobbyist cash smell better.

Amo's legislative output has been modest. GovTrack's 2024 report card noted he received the 10th fewest cosponsors on his bills among House Democrats. His focus areas -- international affairs, armed forces, and law enforcement -- conveniently avoid the populist economic reforms that 80% of Americans support. The man who worked inside the White House for two presidents apparently learned one lesson above all: don't rock the boat that floats your career.

Yassamin Ansari is the freshman Democrat representing Arizona's 3rd congressional district, and she arrived in Washington with a resume that reads like a climate diplomat's fever dream. Before Congress, she served as the youngest woman ever elected to the Phoenix City Council, created the city's Office of Heat Response and Mitigation, and spearheaded a plan to electrify Phoenix's bus fleet by 2040 [1]. Before that, she worked in the Executive Office of UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon on the team that helped deliver the Paris Climate Agreement [2]. She is the first Iranian-American ever elected to public office in Arizona [3]. She serves on the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee and the Natural Resources Committee, where she is Ranking Member of the Energy and Minerals Subcommittee [4].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Ansari is one of the more naturally aligned members of Congress. She joined bipartisan colleagues on the House floor to call for a ban on congressional stock trading, supporting the TRUST in Congress Act that would require members and their families to divest individual holdings [5]. She cosponsored a bipartisan War Powers Resolution to rein in presidential military authority and delivered a floor speech backing it, declaring that "war carries profound and deadly consequences for our troops, for the American people and for the entire world" [6][7]. As a candidate, she joined over 50 House challengers calling on Congress to pass the Freedom to Vote Act, which would end partisan gerrymandering and crack down on dark money [8]. She supports Medicare for All and expanding Medicare's ability to negotiate drug prices, and has called for a tax system that "ensures the top 1%, Wall Street, and large corporations pay their fair share" [9]. She voted YES on the Epstein Files Transparency Act as an original cosponsor and later attended the Les Wexner deposition as a member of the Oversight Committee, accusing the Trump administration of a cover-up when DOJ releases proved incomplete [10][11].

Where Ansari earns particular credit is on accountability and transparency. Her very first piece of original legislation, the Dual Hatting Limitation Act, targeted the revolving door between government and private industry by prohibiting Trump administration officials from holding dual roles [12]. That instinct to police conflicts of interest aligns cleanly with the 80% Bill's calls for a lifetime lobbying ban, the DISCLOSE Act, and banning dark money. Her progressive caucus membership and stated positions suggest she would back paid family leave, Social Security funding, police body cameras, and right to repair, though some of these still await her formal co-sponsorship or floor vote.

Follow the money and you find a fairly clean ledger for a freshman. Her 2024 campaign raised roughly $1.7 million, with top contributors including CDS International Holdings, True North Holdings, and the law firm Snell & Wilmer [13][14]. She received an endorsement from the Congressional Progressive Caucus PAC and EMILY's List [15]. The Democratic Majority for Israel also spent independently on her race, which tracks with her January 2025 vote supporting legislation sanctioning the International Criminal Court over arrest warrants for Israeli leaders, a position that put her at odds with some progressive allies [16]. That ICC vote is worth watching as a potential pressure point where donor relationships may shape positions, but it falls outside the 80% Bill's scope.

The bottom line on Ansari: she is a freshman who talks a strong populist game and has early receipts to show for it. Her votes on stock trading, war powers, the Epstein files, and campaign finance reform put her squarely in 80% Bill territory on the issues where she has had the chance to act. The question is whether the remaining items on the list, from term limits to junk fees to right to repair, will earn her vote when the pressure is on, or whether her record will quietly plateau at "supports in principle."
